name: ivory_dna_marketplace_seller_anonymity_vs_species_task_force
premise: Platform trust lead Casey Diaz must decide whether to comply with a wildlife task force subpoena for seller IPs tied to listings of antique ivory with questionable DNA barcodes. Seller advocate Finn Xu argues premature disclosure burns good-faith collectors and drives sales to dark forums. Refusing slows a transnational task force tracking a cartel using forged provenance. The platform loyalty oath emphasizes protecting seller communities; prosecutors threaten platform liability.

agents.name: Casey Diaz
agents.identity: I am the marketplace trust lead balancing subpoena compliance, seller privacy commitments, and reputational risk if ivory laundering occurs on our rails.
agents.goal: I want law enforcement supported without destroying collector trust in our dispute systems overnight.

agents.name: Finn Xu
agents.identity: I am a seller advocate who represents estate-sale families who inherited ambiguous carvings and who fear wrongful exposure from sloppy DNA matches.
agents.goal: I want a court-supervised narrow disclosure of only flagged transactions after independent lab replication, even if that delays the task force.

choices: I will comply immediately with the subpoena and freeze related listings, accepting seller backlash and possible wrongful freezes until exoneration reviews finish.
I will seek a protective order limiting disclosure to pseudonymous identifiers until DNA labs replicate results next week, accepting obstruction accusations if judges disagree.
forced_choice.call_to_action: Federal prosecutors are on a conference bridge waiting for Casey Diaz's answer. Casey Diaz must choose between immediate subpoena compliance or a protective-order motion first. What does Casey Diaz do?
